coredata.dc:title: Role of conductive channels via CQDs on NiO/g-C3N4 Z-scheme composite as a bi-functional photocatalyst
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Noor, Sunbel
Haider, Rida Shahzadi
Noor, Saima
Sajjad, Shamaila
Khan Leghari, Sajjad Ahmed
Mehboob, Maria
Long, Mingce
coredata.dc:description:
Herein, an innovative graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) based nanocomposite with nickel oxide (NiO) is modified with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) to fabricate CQD/NiO/g-C3N4 ternary composites. The effect of various concentrations of CQDs is investigated for photo electrochemical (PEC) water splitting and photocatalytic degradation of methyl orange (MO). XRD pattern of CQDs/NiO/g-C3N4 composite showed the distinctive peaks of g-C3N4 and NiO. The reduction of band-gap values from 2.62 to 1.8 eV in UV–Visible spectra of nanocomposites confirmed an improvement toward red shift. The existence of required functional groups and successful fabrication of nanosheets of g-C3N4 and its nanocomposites are confirmed by FTIR. PL confirms the decrease in electron-hole recombination in binary and ternary composites. CQDs/NiO/g-C3N4 (4.0 mL) showed enhanced PEC activity and photocatalytic performance in visible light exposure. CQDs/NiO/g-C3N4 (4.0 mL) showed an obvious enhancement in current density of
∼
-19 mAcm−2 at potential 0.39 V during hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) and high current density of
∼
15 mAcm−2 at 1.42 V in oxygen evolution reaction (OER) than all others. The highest degradation rate of MO by CQDs/NiO/g-C3N4 (4.0 mL) was noted due to availability of plenty of active sites and conductive channels provided by CQDs. Incorporated CQDs in samples affect the overall water splitting response and photocatalytic activity by acting as electron conduction bridge for the quick electron transfer from the composite materials to reaction sites on its surface. This combination is active for efficient PEC water splitting and photo degradation purposes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Water splitting
Charge separation
Photo electrochemical reactions
Hydrogen production

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Principle of PEC water splitting on CQDs modified NiO/g-C3N4 photo electrode
Photocatalytic degradation of MO and stability test
Proposed mechanism of degradation of MO

The work is also acknowledged to Pakistan Institute of Engineering and Applied Sciences. We are grateful to Allama Iqbal Open University (AIOU), Institute of Space and Technology (IST) Islamabad for analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) XRD profile and (B) FTIR spectra of pure g-C3N4, NiO/g-C3N4, CQDs/NiO/g-C3N4 with CQDs contents 2.0 mL and 4.0 mL.
SEM images, EDX analysis and elemental mapping of g-C3N4 and CQDs (4.0 mL)/NiO/g-C3N4 composite.
(A) UV–Visible DRS absorbance spectra, (consistent digital photographs of samples) (B) corresponding band gap value of g-C3N4, NiO/g-C3N4, CQDs/NiO/g-C3N4 with CQDs contents 2.0 mL and 4.0 mL.
Photoluminescence spectra of g-C3N4, NiO/g-C3N4, CQDs/NiO/g-C3N4 with CQDs contents 2.0 mL and 4.0 mL at ambient temperature under the exciting wavelength of 330 nm.

(A) Photo electrochemical LSV response of samples in 0.5 M KOH electrolyte at pH
∼
12.3, under λ > 420 nm irradiation and 20 mv/sec. (B) CV response of CQDs/NiO/g-C3N4 with CQDs contents 4.0 mL under visible light irradiation for 5 cycles at scan rate of 20 mv/sec (C) transient photocurrent densities versus time (D) Polarization curves of prepared samples.
(A) Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) Nyquist plots (B) Bode plots of Pure and composite samples.
Time dependent degradation (A), reaction kinetic study (B) by synthesized samples (C) stability test of CQDs/NiO/g-C3N4 (4.0 mL) composite over MO.
Mechanistic approach for PEC water splitting on CQDs modified NiO/g-C3N4 photo electrode.
Mechanistic z-scheme approach for photocatalytic degradation of MO through CQDs/NiO/g-C3N4 composite.
Literature study of PEC and pollutant photodegradation with present work.

row.entry: 40 mV@10 mA cm−2 (on glassy carbon), 200 mV@10 mA cm−2 (on Ni foam)

5 μmol/gh H2 evolution &
2.5 μmol/gh O2 evolution
Electrochemical OER and solar photocatalysis of BPB
H2 evolution reaction, O2 evolution reaction and MO photodegradation

19 mAcm−2 at potential 0.39 V
15 mAcm−2at potential 1.42 V 92.0 % removal

head.ce:title: Role of conductive channels via CQDs on NiO/g-C3N4 Z-scheme composite as a bi-functional photocatalyst

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Pakistan Institute of Engineering and Applied Sciences, Islamabad, Pakistan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Pakistan Institute of Engineering and Applied Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Pakistan Institute of Engineering and Applied Sciences, Islamabad, Pakistan

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Catalysis, Dalian National Laboratory for Clean Energy, Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Zhongshan Road 457, Dalian 116023, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Dalian National Laboratory for Clean Energy

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental Science and Engineering, Key Laboratory of Thin Film and Micro-fabrication Technology, Shanghai Jiao Tong University, Shanghai 200240, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental Science and Engineering
Key Laboratory of Thin Film and Micro-fabrication Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Environmental Science and Engineering, Key Laboratory of Thin Film and Micro-fabrication Technology, Shanghai Jiao Tong University, Shanghai 200240, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. International Islamic University, Islamabad, Pakistan.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Herein, an innovative graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) based nanocomposite with nickel oxide (NiO) is modified with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) to fabricate CQD/NiO/g-C3N4 ternary composites. The effect of various concentrations of CQDs is investigated for photo electrochemical (PEC) water splitting and photocatalytic degradation of methyl orange (MO). XRD pattern of CQDs/NiO/g-C3N4 composite showed the distinctive peaks of g-C3N4 and NiO. The reduction of band-gap values from 2.62 to 1.8 eV in UV–Visible spectra of nanocomposites confirmed an improvement toward red shift. The existence of required functional groups and successful fabrication of nanosheets of g-C3N4 and its nanocomposites are confirmed by FTIR. PL confirms the decrease in electron-hole recombination in binary and ternary composites. CQDs/NiO/g-C3N4 (4.0 mL) showed enhanced PEC activity and photocatalytic performance in visible light exposure. CQDs/NiO/g-C3N4 (4.0 mL) showed an obvious enhancement in current density of
∼
-19 mAcm−2 at potential 0.39 V during hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) and high current density of
∼
15 mAcm−2 at 1.42 V in oxygen evolution reaction (OER) than all others. The highest degradation rate of MO by CQDs/NiO/g-C3N4 (4.0 mL) was noted due to availability of plenty of active sites and conductive channels provided by CQDs. Incorporated CQDs in samples affect the overall water splitting response and photocatalytic activity by acting as electron conduction bridge for the quick electron transfer from the composite materials to reaction sites on its surface. This combination is active for efficient PEC water splitting and photo degradation purposes.

ce:section.ce:para: Currently global warming, ever-growing energy consumption and environmental waste water treatment are the most important problems which are affecting the modern human community. To overcome these challenges, researchers and scientists have paid their attentions towards the growth of alternative energy resources that will replace conventional toxic and non-renewable energy sources [1–3]. Hydrogen production through PEC water splitting is an alternative way for ecological and sustainable energy source. As secondary energy carrier hydrogen appears as favourable, eco and environmentally friendly [4]. As the water is abundant source of hydrogen on the earth so hydrogen generation through PEC water splitting is an objective for energy science and technology [5].

Many photocatalysts with their distinct and exceptional properties i.e., the band gap energies, conduction and valence band potentials along with thermo-chemical stability have got significance in process of solar hydrogen generation. Moreover, abundant earth materials have been approachable choice for making potential and influential photocatalyst in vital applications [6–9].

The g-C3N4 is active allotrope amongst numerous carbon nitrides. Firstly, Berzelius and Liebig in 1830 [10,11] found g-C3N4 in primary form “Melon” which is a right polymer comprising intersected tri-s-triazines via secondary nitrogens. Instead it is a 2D-material comprising of tri-s-triazines through tertiary amines [12]. In 2006 it was introduced concerning heterogeneous catalysis but for light induced photocatalytic water splitting it had been reported by Wang at el [13]. g-C3N4 owns energy band gap value of 2.7 eV with the conduction and valence band edge potential of
∼ −
1.1 eV and
+
1.6 eV/NHE, respectively [14] that enables it an active photocatalyst. Its two components carbon (C) and nitrogen (N) are abundant elements on earth. It makes it to be prepared effortlessly and cost-effectively. Its polymeric nature has not only the ability to control the surface chemistry through molecular level alteration but also ensures the surface elasticity of the structure. Thus, it is most favourable and encouraging photocatalyst for environmental clean-up and water splitting applications due to its distinctive abovementioned features and individualities [15].

While poor charge separation of photogenerated charge carriers of g-C3N4 reduces its photocatalytic efficiency [16]. As a result to inhibit recombination process and increase its photocatalytic efficiency, it should be coupled with numerous diverse and reactive materials [17]. As NiO is a p-type semiconductor, it can perform as an appropriate photocatalyst under UV light owning to have the distinctive characteristic i.e., low price, prominent chemical and enduring catalytic stability [18,19]. Chen et al. [20] have used NiO/InVO4 photocatalysts for visible light application. Fu Yijun et al. [21] demonstrated the overall water splitting through amorphous NiO incorporated on g-C3N4 without usage of any sacrificial reagent. In the coupling system of g-C3N4 with NiO charge carrier separation can be improved proficiently leading to enhancement of H2 evolution rate. But wide band gap of NiO (
∼
3.5 eV) reduces its photocatalytic activity and slows down redox reactions. Therefore, to improve photocatalytic performance, CQDs play an important role. As CQDs have been extensively used for photocatalyst design on account of their outstanding individualities, e.g., strong solubility, size effect and good charge transfer properties [22]. It diversifies the conduction band of g-C3N4. S. Feng et al. [23] have observed photocatalytic removal of RhB, MB and phenol by employing CdS/CQDs/g-C3N4 composites under visible-near-infrared light through Z-scheme. G. Liu et al. [24] have reported on successful 98% removal of perfluoroalkyl carboxylic acids (PFOA) polluted wastewater through bimetallic CNi–Al2O3 structure in 3 h.

So herein, we developed cost-effective and light weight graphitic carbon nitride nanocomposites (CQDs/NiO/g-C3N4) for the improvement of PEC and photodegradation of MO. Incorporated CQDs affect the overall water splitting response even at lower potentials. CQDs enhance the PEC performance by attaining the stability at higher potentials and under electrolyte. Decrease in photocurrent and phocatalytic activity in CQDs/NiO/g-C3N4 (2.0 mL) photocatalyst may happen due to less active sites to be exposed to take part in PEC water splitting reaction. However, it was further utilized for investigation of photo electrocatalytic overall water splitting performance.

Thiourea (CH4N2S, assay
>
96.0%), urea (CH4N2O, assay
≅
98.5%), nickel chloride (NiCl2 purity
≥
99.0%), ethanol (C2H5OH, 99.08% minimum assay), ammonia (NH3 solution 32.0%) and citric acid (C6H8O7, 99.5% pure) were used. These chemicals were bought from Sigma-Aldrich and used to synthesize materials without further refinement. Distilled water has been used all over the experiments.

Graphitic carbon nitride has been synthesized through mixing urea (3.0 g) and thiourea (3.0 g) into a clean alumina crucible. To limit the amount of gas loss, the crucible is covered and tightly wrapped with aluminium foil to avoid air contact. Thermal treatment was performed in oven at 400 °C for 1 h. The obtained yellowish colour powder was assembled and exercised for the synthesis of nanocomposites.
For CQDs citric acid (1.5 g) was dissolved in water (80 mL). This mixture was transferred to Teflon lined autoclave under hydrothermal treatment for 3 h at 160 °C.

Co-precipitation technique was used to synthesis 2D-nanosheets of NiO. Firstly, under constant stirring for half an hour nickel chloride (4.0 g) was liquefied into distilled water (150 mL) and then aqueous ammonia (10.0 mL) was added dropwise into above solution. As pH intensely effects the morphology of sample. So Ni(OH)2 nanosheets in the form of green precipitates have been produced.

When aqueous ammonia is added to maintain pH a weak alkaline solution is achieved and this solution contributes hydroxide ions for production of Ni(OH)2. The whole phenomenon is described below (Eq. 1-5). For the production of [Ni(NH3)6]2+ ammonia perform as an intricate reagent for Ni2+ ions as well as for the formation of Ni(OH) nanosheets, [Ni(NH3)6]2+ perform a substantial character by directing the reaction of Ni2+ with
OH −
[30,31]. To eradicate contaminations and maintain pH, the acquired Ni(OH)2 precipitates have been separated out via centrifugation. It was later washed and dried at 60 °C. Annealing of Ni(OH)2 at high temperature (400 °C) transformed into NiO nanosheets (Eq. (6).) Through annealing, the elimination of hydroxide phase that might present in the key layer of NiO takes place [25].
(1) NiCl.6H2O + H2O ↔ Ni (OH)2 + HCl + 5H2O
(2) Ni (OH)2 + 6NH4(OH) ↔ [Ni(NH3)6]2+ + 6H2O + 2OH−
(3) [Ni (NH3)6]2+ ↔ Ni2+ + 6NH3
(4) 6NH3 + H2O ↔ 6NH4 (OH)
(5) Ni2+ + 2OH− ↔ Ni (OH)2
(6) Ni (OH)2 → NiO + H2O

CQDs/NiO/g-C3N4 nanocomposites were prepared by wet chemical method. Firstly, g-C3N4 (1.5 g) was dissolved into ethanol (10 mL) and ultrasonicated for 1 h. Secondly, 0.06 g NiO was dissolved in 5 mL ethanol. Both these solutions were blended with constantly stir for almost 30 min by keeping g-C3N4 as base. 2.0 mL CQDs were then added drop-wise and stirred this solution for 30 min and we obtained CQDs/NiO/g-C3N4 nanocomposites. Then 4.0 mL CQDs were added drop-wise in NiO/g-C3N4 solution and CQDs/NiO/g-C3N4 nanocomposites were achieved. The systematic route of CQDs/NiO/g-C3N4 nanocomposite is described in Scheme 1.

The Rigaku D/Max diffractometer (Cu kα irradiation, λ = 1.5 Å) functioned at 40 KV and 100 mA used in determining the crystalline structure of catalysts. UV–Vis absorbance spectra of synthesized samples were determined by SHIMADZU (2700) spectrometer. Morphology and sample composition was examined through SEM and EDS (JEOL-JAD 2300), respectively. FTIR spectra of samples were collected by FTIR spectrometer Bruker ALPHA-II. The PL spectra of samples were examined by using fluorescence spectrometer (Perkin Elemer LS-50 B).

The photoelectrochemical measurements were taken by using Corrtest potentiostat equipped with three-electrode system. Standard calomel electrode (SCE) as reference and platinum (pt) as counter electrode were employed. Applied potential measured with SCE is converted into RHE. For working electrode catalyst solutions were made by mixing 1.0 mg samples in 10 mL ethanol and homogenized under ultra-sonication for 1 h and made thin films on FTO. Through Linear Sweep Voltammetry (LSV) polarization curves were determined at 0.3 V (vs. NHE) in 0.5 M KOH electrolyte maintain at neutral PH
∼
12.3 and under λ > 420 nm irradiation. The photo-induced electrochemical CV reaction was examined under visible light irradiation for 5 cycles at scan rate of 20 mv/sec. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) performance was evaluated in 0.5 M KOH electrolyte maintain at neutral pH
∼
12.3 under λ > 420 nm irradiation.

Samples were further tested on photocatalytic degradation of MO dye in 100 mL quartz photochemical reactor under illumination of 1000 W Xenon lamp. 20 ppm solution of MO was made. 5.0 mg catalyst was dissolved into 50 mL of MO dye solution and stir for half an hour in absence of light for adsorption-desorption equilibrium. After that each 4.0 mL exposed solution were taken at regular interval of 1 h, centrifuged and absorption spectra of MO was determined by UV–Visible spectrometer SHIMADZU (2700). Same steps were repeated for remaining samples.

For crystallographic information of the synthesized photocatalysts XRD patterns of g-C3N4, NiO/g-C3N4 and CQDs modified NiO/g-C3N4 were analysed and depicted in Fig. 1 A. In the XRD profile of g-C3N4 a prominent signals at 27.4° (002) and comparatively a small intensity peak at 12.9° (100) indicating the inter-planar stacking of conjugated aromatic rings (graphitic materials) and repeating units of periodic tri-s-triazine, respectively well agreed with literature [26,27]. The diffraction peaks of NiO/g-C3N4 and CQDs/NiO/g-C3N4 (2.0 mL of CQDs) not only have the signals of g-C3N4 but also diffraction peaks of FCC NiO at the 37.28° (111), 43.30° (200) and 62.78° (220) [JCPDS-71-1179] [28]. However, the peak intensities of NiO is very weak and even not detected in CQDs/NiO/g-C3N4 with amount of 4.0 mL CQDs might be due to low content or poor diffraction signals of NiO. It is clearly observed in all composite structures that (100) characteristic peak of g-C3N4 has been shifted towards the lower angle (10.9°) and become more sharp as compared to pristine g-C3N4. Z. Zhang et al. have observed the same behaviour when they integrated ZnIn2S4 onto g-C3N4 sheets [29]. This is because that some metal ions of NiO might be interacted with g-C3N4 by the electrons lone-pair of nitrogen results into the enhancement of the intra planar structural units of tri-s-triazine. Moreover no diffraction peaks of CQDs were detected in both CQDs/NiO/g-C3N4 (with 2.0 and 4.0 mL CQDs) samples because of its low contents but the intensity of main peak (002) of g-C3N4 is slightly reduced.

Fig. 1B displays the FTIR to understand the existence of functional groups in g-C3N4, NiO/g-C3N4 and composites of CQDs/NiO/g-C3N4. In all samples the characteristic signals appeared at 766 and 890 cm−1 exhibit vibration modes of triazine units and heptazine rings of graphitic carbon nitride, respectively [30]. A series of peaks range between 1540 and 1400 cm−1 are attributed to C–N stretching vibration modes. The peak located at 1540 cm−1 is associated to C
=
N stretching vibrations [31]. The characteristic peak centred at 2363 cm−1 is assigned to H2O molecules present on the surface of samples [32]. A broad range of peaks between the 3376–2830 cm−1 indicate N–H stretching mode [33]. It is observed that pure g-C3N4 and its composites have the quite same spectra with very small variation of peak intensities as observed in XRD analysis which indicates that foreign materials slightly distort chemical structure of g-C3N4.

The surface morphology of photocatalysts was observed by SEM images. Fig. 2 indicates SEM, EDX and elemental mapping of pure g-C3N4 and CQDs (4.0 mL)/NiO/g-C3N4 composite, respectively. Pure g-C3N4 shows irregular non uniform aggregated 2D sheet like structure which is well agreed with reported literature [34,35]. After adding CQDs and NiO in (4.0 mL)/NiO/g-C3N4 composite, it is obviously seen the large stacked sheets like structure with irregular dimensions which specifies the strong anchoring effect of CQDs and NiO. NiO is consistently scattered on the nanosheet because there are no definite nanoparticles on the g-C3N4 framework. CQDs are very hard to identify in SEM photograph due to small size.

The elemental composition of pure g-C3N4 and CQDs (4.0 mL)/NiO/g-C3N4 composite is studied by EDX analysis. EDX analysis of g-C3N4 showed occurrence of C, N and O while CQDs (4.0 mL)/NiO/g-C3N4 has C, N, O and Ni elements. However in both samples the signals of W also observed which was used during sputtering of samples.

Moreover, the elemental mapping images illustrate the elements distribution of g-C3N4 and CQDs/NiO/g-C3N4 composite. Red, yellow and green colours represent the presence of C, N and O, respectively, in both samples. While orange colour indicates Ni element present in ternary composite which was agreed with EDX results.

UV–Visible DRS absorption spectroscopy of photocatalysts measures the optical response is presented in Fig. 3 A. It is observed in spectrum of g-C3N4 and NiO/g-C3N4 broad absorption peaks cantered at 249 and 298 nm is associated to π–π∗ electron transition of aromatic conjugated system and n–π∗ electron transitions from a nonbonding orbital to an aromatic anti-bonding orbital (CN), respectively. The absorption tail of g-C3N4 (
∼
474 nm) is broadening to visible region. By the incorporation of NiO into g-C3N4 very small absorption in visible region is observed due to having the large band gap value (
∼
3.5 eV) of NiO [36]. But NiO promote the electron transition by electrostatic forces between the interfaces of NiO/g-C3N4 which reduce the chances of recombination process. The absorption edge of both CQDs/NiO/g-C3N4 with 2.0 mL and 4.0 mL CQDs show the noticeable absorption edge towards the longer wavelength by integration of CQDs. The position of CB edge of g-C3N4 highly negative (−1.3 eV vs. NHE) [37] which is not suitable for generation of hydrogen. By the introduction of CQDs optical properties of g-C3N4 can be enhanced because excessive orbitals are provided by sp2 carbon atoms [38,39]. Moreover CQDs performance as electron conduction bridge for the quick electron transfer from the composite materials to reaction sites on its surface. Fig. 3B shows the corresponding band gap value of g-C3N4, NiO/g-C3N4, CQDs/NiO/g-C3N4 with CQDs contents 2.0 mL and 4.0 mL measured by Tauc relation and found to be 2.62, 2.5, 2.3 and 1.8 eV, respectively.

The photoluminescence (PL) spectra of photocatalysts further helps to govern the investigation of charge separation and recombination process, emission mechanism and variation in surface states, as displayed in Fig. 4 . In PL spectrum of g-C3N4 two prominent emission peaks are observed at 439 and 479 nm indicating electronic transition through sp3 C–Nσ (2.98 eV) and sp2 C–Nπ (2.7 eV) well matched with reported literature [40]. By the incorporation of NiO and CDQs the reduction in PL intensity and little PL peak shifting has observed. However, the shifting in PL intensity has been observed by incorporating CDQs on NiO/g-C3N4 nanocomposites and significant quenching in emission peak intensity is also obviously seen in CQDs/NiO/g-C3N4 (4.0 mL CQDs), leading to π-π interactions under type-II van der Waals hetero-junction between CQDs and NiO/g-C3N4 which is helpful in reduction of recombination process. However, PL emission peak maxima of CQDs/NiO/g-C3N4 (2.0 mL CQDs) experience a bathochromic shift from 479 to 483 nm which possibly due to the presence of sub-gap defects in sample introduced by incorporated materials [41].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle of PEC water splitting on CQDs modified NiO/g-C3N4 photo electrode
ce:section.ce:para:
PEC water splitting cell was exploited for water splitting reaction to investigate the photocatalyst performance. The probes are typically plunged into an electrolyte (e.g. aqueous solution of 0.5 M KOH, adjusted pH
∼
12.3) and linked via an external circuit. Throughout the process OER and HER occurred on the surfaces of the photoanode and the cathode, respectively. On the exposure of incident light on the photoanode, electrons from VB migrate to CB of NiO/g-C3N4 generate the photoelectrons which further move to from CB of NiO to VB of g-C3N4 and to surface of CQDs for space charge separation under z-scheme mechanism (completely explained in Scheme 2). CQDs play the role as electron acceptor and donor which provide the stability and improved visible light response. Photogenerated holes in VB of NiO undergo the oxidation reaction. Consequently, the photo generated electrons move from the photo anode to the cathode via the external circuit and afterwards lessen the protons dissolved in the electrolyte and produce HER at cathode in Eq. (7)
2H++2e−→H2 [Ered0 = 0V vs. NHE] where Redo is H2 evolution potential (7)

At the intervening period the holes exited in the valence band of photocatalyst from inside the photoanode to the external surface and oxidize water to generate OER, as described in Eq. (8)
2H2O+4h+→4H++O2 [Eox0 = 1.23V vs. NHE] where Aux o is O2 evolution potential (8)

Aside from the ones which are individually engaged in the electrode reactions the remaining photo generated electrons and holes are unable to arrive at the photo-electrode surface throughout their relocation process or will be reconstituted and nullified as described in Scheme 2
.

PEC activities were performed to determine the overall water splitting of synthesized photocatalysts. Fig. 5 A indicates LSV curves (transient photocurrent densities vs. RHE) of g-C3N4, NiO/g-C3N4, CQDs/NiO/g-C3N4 with CQDs contents 2.0 mL and 4.0 mL at scan rate of 20 mV/s by mean of standard three-electrode setup under exposure of visible light (λ > 420). It is seen that photocathode of g-C3N4 show the current density
∼ −
7.8 mAcm−2 at potential 0.38 V. A very different behaviour of NiO/g-C3N4 composite was observed which is catalytically inactive in HER that Ni2+ possibly delay the reduction process. By incorporation of CQDs an improved current density was observed by CQDs/NiO/g-C3N4 (2.0 mL). While an obvious enhancement in current density of
∼
-19 mAcm−2 at potential 0.39 V through CQDs/NiO/g-C3N4 (4.0 mL) was depicted. CQDs offer emissive traps by π-π interaction and quantum confinement effect which improve the reduction properties of samples by reducing the recombination rate of photoinduced charge carriers as observed through PL analysis [41]. J. W. Shi et al. [42] have reported notable photogenerated H2 evolution rate (5.04 μmol h−1) by NiO/Ni2P/CN under exposure of visible-light. In the reported literature it is observed [43] that CdS/vanadium carbide composite simultaneously produced highest rate of H2 (20.5 mmol g−1 h−1) with selective oxidation of benzyl alcohol into benzaldehyde which was
∼
661 times greater than the pristine CdS nanowires. In OER g-C3N4 and NiO/g-C3N4 show very poor activity which might be due to accumulation of H2O2 which deteriorated the surface of catalysts and reduce the current densities. A very slight enhancement of current density was observed in CQDs/NiO/g-C3N4 (2.0 mL). CQDs/NiO/g-C3N4 (4.0 mL) composite showed high current density of
∼
15 mAcm−2 at 1.42 V. Equally photoinduced holes on the surface of g-C3N4 oxidize water to form H2O2 and O2. CQDs provide the additional path and supress rate of H2O2 molecules [44].

The recyclability of the CQDs/NiO/g-C3N4 (4.0 mL) composite is shown in Fig. 5B. The photocatalyst is subjected to cyclic voltammetry measurements up to 5-cycles under visible light at scan rate of 20 mv/sec. Continuous cycles show increase in current density indicating visible light activation and stability of sample. M. Danish et al. [45] have shown the better electrochemical performance of Mn3O4 nanoparticles among MnO, MnO2 and Mn2O3 through CV.

Fig. 5C indicates the photocurrent-time curves of prepared samples in dark and visible light switching mode. It is obvious from spectra that CQDs/NiO/g-C3N4 (4.0 mL) composite has stronger photocurrent density than all others which indicates more production of photogenerated electrons and high separation rate of photogenerated electron−hole pairs also seen in PL experiment. This property is responsible for better performance of sample in PEC and photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutant.

Further to verify the stability of incorporated NiO in photocatalyst, the potentio-dynamic polarization curves were obtained to calculate the corrosion resistance of g-C3N4, NiO/g-C3N4, CQDs/NiO/g-C3N4 with CQDs contents 2.0 mL and 4.0 mL photoanodes, as shown in Fig. 5D. It is observed that the corrosion potentials (Ecorr) of pure g-C3N4, g–C3N4–NiO and CQDs decorated NiO/g-C3N4 composite were negatively shifted. CQDs/NiO/g-C3N4 (4.0 mL) and CQDs/NiO/g-C3N4 (2.0 mL) composites display Ecorr value of 0.57 V and 0.38 V, correspondingly. The corrosion current density (Icorr) of g-C3N4 and NiO/g-C3N4 is lessened. Contrast with that of CQDs decorated g–C3N4–NiO composite Icorr of CQDs/NiO/g-C3N4 (2.0 mL) only slightly increased, whereas CQDs/NiO/g-C3N4 (4.0 mL) has significantly increased. The estimated polarization resistance (Rp) indicates similar fashion and CQDs/NiO/g-C3N4 (4.0 mL) has demonstrated the highest possible Rp value of 57 × 105 Ω cm2 which indicated enhanced corrosion resistance. While CQDs/NiO/g-C3N4 (2.0 mL) composite has Rp = 38 × 105 Ω cm2. The above polarization tests showed that CQDs/NiO/g-C3N4 (4.0 mL) photocatalyst exhibited the improved corrosion resistance as compared to others.

The EIS measurement has been conducted at the open-circuit voltage under visible light illumination. Fig. 6 A represents the Nyquist plots of samples. It is observed that CQDs/NiO/g-C3N4 (4.0 mL) has small value of arc radius than all other samples which reduce charge carrier transfer impedance between electrode/electrolyte interfaces [32]. It is depicted from Bode plot (Fig. 6B) that CQDs/NiO/g-C3N4 (4.0 mL) has small value of fmax leading to large life time of charge carriers which further delay the recombination process at electrode/electrolyte.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic degradation of MO and stability test
ce:section.ce:para:
To examine the performance of photocatalyst its application for photocatalytic degradation of MO was also conducted under the exposure of visible light after 5 h as illustrated in Fig. 7 . Pure g-C3N4 degraded only 37% of MO. No doubt g-C3N4 is very active catalyst but poor activity was ascribed to fast recombination process of electron-hole pairs. By coupling g-C3N4 with NiO photoactivity against MO 48% was recorded. NiO acts as electrons sink and provide the active sites for adsorption of pollutant to increase the photocatlytic process. K. Qi et al. [14] have recorded 74.0% photodegradation of the tetracycline hydrochloride antibiotic by 10%-NiO/g-C3N4 nanocomposite in 2 h. Removal of MO through CQDs/NiO/g-C3N4 with CQDs contents 2.0 mL and 4.0 mL was observed
∼
81.0 and
∼
92.0%, respectively. Highest degradation rate of MO by CQDs/NiO/g-C3N4 (4.0 mL) is attributed to high surface area and availability of conductive channels provided by CQDs for the passage of charge carriers to surface of composites. This synergism between CQDs and NiO/g-C3N4 significantly improves response of photocatalyst.

The time degradation graph of MO by prepared material is shown in Fig. 7B follows pseudo first order kinetics by employing eq. (9).
(9) ln (Co/C) = kappt

Here Co (t = 0) is initial and C is solute remaining concentration, respectively. kapp is degradation rate constant. It is perceived that CQDs/NiO/g-C3N4 (4.0 mL) shows the highest kapp against MO. Fig. 7C shows recycling (4 cycles) of CQDs/NiO/g-C3N4 (4.0 mL) against degradation of MO. It is noted only 9.0% loss during degradation process which indicates that sample is highly stable and could be exploited for long time in industrial applications. Literature study of PEC and pollutant photodegradation with present work is compared in Table 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Proposed mechanism of degradation of MO
ce:section.ce:para:
Valence
(
E VB
)
and conduction band edge potential
(
E CB
)
are very important for photodegradation mechanism of catalyst and measured through following equations 10 and 11.
(10)
E VB
= χ −
E e
+
0.5. E
g
(11)
E CB
=
E VB
−
E g

Here
χ ,
E e
and
E g
represents electronegativity, free electron energy (4.5eV vs. NHE) and band gap energy of sample, respectively. The value of
χ
is determined by equation (12) [54].
(12)
χ =
[ x
( A )
a
. x
( B )
b
]
1
a + b

Calculated value of
χ
for g-C3N4 and NiO is 4.73 and 5.75 eV, respectively. The measured
E VB
of g-C3N4 and NiO is
+
1.54 and
+
3.01, while
E CB
of g-C3N4 and NiO is
−
1.08 and
−
0.49 eV vs. NHE, respectively.
E g
of g-C3N4 is found 2.62 eV by Tauc relation whereas NiO is 3.5 eV according to literature [36]. The proposed photocatalytic degradation mechanism of MO through CQDs/NiO/g-C3N4 composite is shown through z–scheme approach (Scheme 3). Upon the exposure of visible light electrons of g-C3N4/NiO are excited from VB to CB. CQDs play the curial role of electrons shuttling between g-C3N4 and NiO because of its small size and high electron conductivity. CQDs are both donor/acceptor and responsible for shifting of electrons from CB of NiO to VB of g-C3N4 which possibly create the space separation and accumulation of electrons of g-C3N4 and holes of NiO [55,56]. Which effectively reduce the chances of recombination process of freshly generated charge carriers [57]. Consequently, electrons from CB of g-C3N4 are trapped by dissolved O2 of pollutant solution to harvest superoxide radicals
(
O 2
• −
)
under reduction reaction. As
E CB
of g-C3N4 is more negative than redox potential
O 2
/
O 2
• −
(
−
0.33eV vs. NHE) [54]. Whereas
E VB
of NiO is greater than standard redox potential of
OH •
/
OH −
(
+
2.4eV. vs. NHE) [54] very appropriate for oxidation process and convert H2O to
OH −
/
OH •
. These photogenerated radicals
O 2
• −
/
OH •
have enough potential to oxidize the adsorbed molecules of MO and convert it into less harmful compounds [58].

In summary, we have introduced effective synthesis strategy for CQDs/NiO/g-C3N4 composite by hydrothermal method and their absorption at higher wavelengths and the light-induced charge carriers separation efficiency/mobility enhanced remarkably. Crucial charge carrier recombination step decides the fate of photocatalytic reaction and under visible light irradiation (λ > 420), the photoelectrocatalytic response of optimized CQDs/NiO/g-C3N4 (4.0 mL) (j = 15 mAcm−2) is stable throughout the PEC process in contrast to the pristine one, because of an improved charge separation and fast hole extraction. Concomitantly, optimal deposition of CQDs on NiO/g-C3N4 enhance the chemical stability for NiO incorporated photocatalysts and improve the PEC performance even at higher energy potentials. Moreover, the highest degradation performance of MO by CQDs/NiO/g-C3N4 (4.0 mL) added advantage to the PEC results. This work exploits the electron mediator/transfer property of CQDs between interface of CQDs and NiO/g-C3N4 improving charge separation efficiency and synergistic effects between CQDs and NiO/g-C3N4 significantly enhance photocatalytic response. Utilizing the concept of decorating NiO/g-C3N4 with CQDs for improved stability and visible light response may be applied for constructing novel CQDs coupled with C3N4 frameworks and chemically unstable semiconductors for PEC reactions to further improve their photoelectron catalytic performance.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work is supported by International Islamic University, Islamabad, Higher Education Commission of Pakistan (NRPU 3660). The work is also acknowledged to Pakistan Institute of Engineering and Applied Sciences. We are grateful to Allama Iqbal Open University (AIOU), Institute of Space and Technology (IST) Islamabad for analysis.
